content:
Every Angular codebase has one. You know exactly which component I’m talking about. It’s the file people hesitate to open. The one that makes you pause before making even a small change because you’re never quite sure what else might break. What’s interesting is that these components rarely start out as disasters. In fact, they usually start out as examples of good code.
I’ve watched this happen across multiple projects over the years, and if I’m honest, I’ve contributed to a few of these monsters myself. The story almost always begins the same way. A developer creates a clean, focused component.
It’s around a hundred lines long. It has a couple of @Input() properties, maybe a single service call, and a straightforward template. The code is easy to follow, reviewers are happy, and everyone agrees it's well designed.
Fast forward six or eight months. That same component has grown to nearly two thousand lines. Every sprint adds another requirement. Another API call. Another button. Another dialog. Another condition that “only belongs on this page.” Nobody planned for it to become a problem. It simply grew, one reasonable feature request at a time. Eventually, the component earns a reputation. During sprint planning someone inevitably says,
